section.title: Mourning for the One They Pierced
section.sectionRationale: The second burden opens on the day of the LORD. The nations gather against Jerusalem, but the God who stretches out the heavens makes the city an immovable stone that shatters all who lift it. Then, at the heart of the deliverance, comes the most piercing line of the book: the LORD pours out a spirit of grace, and His people look on Me, the One they have pierced, and mourn. John hears the cross in these words.
section.thesis: The LORD who defends Jerusalem against the nations wounds His people more deeply with grace than the sword ever could, opening their eyes to the One they pierced so that they mourn in repentance and are saved.
section.frameNote: Two movements: Jerusalem made an immovable stone that breaks the gathered nations, the weakest defender like David and the house of David like God; and the outpoured spirit of grace that makes the city look on the pierced One and mourn, clan by clan, for an only Son.

units.label: Jerusalem, an immovable stone
units.note: the nations broken on the city of God

verses.text: This is the burden of the word of the LORD concerning Israel. Thus declares the LORD, who stretches out the heavens and lays the foundation of the earth, who forms the spirit of man within him:
“Behold, I will make Jerusalem a cup of drunkenness to all the surrounding peoples. Judah will be besieged, as well as Jerusalem.
On that day, when all the nations of the earth gather against her, I will make Jerusalem a {{even|heavy stone}} for all the peoples; all who would heave it away will be severely injured.

On that day, declares the LORD, I will strike every horse with panic, and every rider with madness. I will keep a watchful eye on the house of Judah, but I will strike with blindness all the horses of the nations.
Then the leaders of Judah will say in their hearts: ‘The people of Jerusalem are my strength, for the LORD of Hosts is their God.’

On that day I will make the clans of Judah like a firepot in a woodpile, like a flaming torch among the sheaves; they will consume all the peoples around them on the right and on the left, while the people of Jerusalem remain secure there.
The LORD will save the tents of Judah first, so that the glory of the house of David and of the people of Jerusalem may not be greater than that of Judah.
On that day the LORD will defend the people of Jerusalem, so that the weakest among them will be like David, and the house of David will be like God, like the angel of the LORD going before them.
So on that day I will set out to destroy all the nations that come against Jerusalem.

units.label: Mourning the One they pierced
units.note: a spirit of grace and bitter repentance

verses.text: Then I will pour out on the house of David and on the people of Jerusalem a spirit of {{chen|grace}} and prayer, and they will look on Me, the One they have {{daqar|pierced}}. They will mourn for Him as one mourns for an {{yachid|only child}}, and grieve bitterly for Him as one grieves for a firstborn son.
On that day the wailing in Jerusalem will be as great as the wailing of Hadad-rimmon in the plain of Megiddo.
The land will mourn, each clan on its own: the clan of the house of David and their wives, the clan of the house of Nathan and their wives,
the clan of the house of Levi and their wives, the clan of Shimei and their wives,
and all the remaining clans and their wives.

even.gloss: A heavy or burdensome stone. The picture is of a boulder too great to lift; every nation that tries to heave Jerusalem away only ruptures itself in the effort. The city is not strong in itself but is made immovable by the God who forms it, so that the weight of the world's hostility falls back on those who raise it.

chen.gloss: Grace, favor, unmerited kindness. The LORD pours out a spirit of grace and prayer, a Spirit-wrought softening that turns hard hearts toward repentance. It is grace that opens the eyes, grace that produces the mourning; the tears of verse 10 are not the root of salvation but its fruit, drawn out by the freely given favor of God.

daqar.gloss: To pierce or thrust through, as with a sword or spear. The stunning offense of the verse is that the LORD says they pierced Me, yet then mourn for Him: the God of Israel identifies Himself with the pierced One. John 19:37 cites this word of the spear thrust into the crucified Jesus, reading the pierced LORD of Zechariah as the Son on the cross.

yachid.gloss: An only one, an only child, the sole and beloved heir. The same word describes Isaac, Abraham's only son. The mourning for the pierced One is the deepest grief a family can know, the loss of the only child and the firstborn, a sorrow the New Testament sees answered in the only begotten Son given up for sinners.

basic.context: The second of Zechariah's two closing burdens turns to the day of the LORD. The nations of the earth mass against Jerusalem, but God makes the city a cup that makes her attackers stagger and a stone that crushes all who try to lift it. He strikes the enemy cavalry with panic and blindness, and He makes the weakest defender fight like David. Then, in the middle of the rescue, comes the turn no one expects: God pours out a spirit of grace, and the people look on the One they pierced and weep for Him like parents weeping over an only child, family by family, across the whole land.
basic.hermeneutics: This is prophecy of the day of the LORD, wrapped in the recurring phrase on that day. Some of it looks to God's protection of His people in history, but its center, the pierced One and the mourning, is read by the New Testament as fulfilled in Christ. The great debate is whether the chapter describes a still future turning of ethnic Israel to Jesus, or the ingathering of God's people more broadly; either way, the piercing points to the cross.
basic.originalLanguages: Jerusalem is made an even ma'amasah, a heavy stone that injures all who heave it. Salvation comes when God pours out chen, grace, a Spirit-given favor that opens blind eyes. They look on the One they daqar, pierced, the very word John uses of the spear in Jesus' side, and they mourn for Him as for a yachid, an only child, the word used of Isaac, Abraham's only son.
basic.typology: The pierced One mourned as an only child prefigures Christ, the only begotten Son, pierced on the cross and looked upon in faith. The immovable stone that breaks the nations anticipates the stone the builders rejected, which became the cornerstone and the crushing stone of judgment. The outpoured spirit of grace looks ahead to Pentecost and the Spirit who convicts the world of sin.
basic.christ: John 19:37 quotes this verse over the crucified Jesus: they will look on the One they have pierced. Zechariah's astonishing grammar, where the LORD says they pierced Me yet then mourn for Him, is answered in the incarnation: the God of Israel was pierced in the flesh of His Son. The mourning for an only child is the sorrow of those who see what they did to the beloved Son, and it is met not with wrath but with an opened fountain in the next chapter.
basic.god: God is the Creator who stretches out the heavens and forms the human spirit, and He bends all of that power to defend His people and to save them. But His deepest work is not the shattering of the nations; it is the pouring out of grace that breaks hard hearts. He wounds His people with mercy, granting them the gift of repentance, and He so identifies with the pierced One that their sin against Him becomes the doorway to their salvation.
basic.crossReferences: John 19:37 cites the piercing, fulfilled at the cross. Revelation 1:7 says every eye will see Him, including those who pierced Him, and all will mourn. Romans 11 speaks of a future turning of Israel to Christ. Acts 2 shows a crowd in Jerusalem cut to the heart and calling on the Lord.
basic.oneStory: The nations rage against the city of God, but God defends her and, better still, saves her by grace. The turning point of history is a look at the pierced One and a flood of repentant tears. That look is fixed at Golgotha, where the God of Israel, in His Son, is pierced for our transgressions, and where the mourning of the guilty becomes the beginning of their joy.

context.literary: The opening of the second burden, chapters 12 to 14, which unfolds the day of the LORD in three great sweeps. The refrain on that day structures the whole. Chapter 12 moves from the siege and rescue of Jerusalem to the mourning over the pierced One, which flows directly into the opened fountain of chapter 13 and the final reign of chapter 14.
context.historical: Zechariah writes to a small, vulnerable community back from exile, rebuilding a modest Jerusalem under the shadow of great empires. To such people the promise that their city would become an immovable stone breaking the nations, and that God Himself would be pierced and mourned, stretched far beyond their present and pointed to a decisive future act of God.
context.canonical: Zechariah 12:10 is one of the most important messianic texts in the Old Testament, quoted in John 19:37 and echoed in Revelation 1:7. It joins Isaiah 53 and Psalm 22 in foretelling a pierced and suffering deliverer, and it grounds the New Testament hope of a repentant looking to Christ that brings salvation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure a fearful people that God will defend His city against all the nations, and to reveal that the heart of that deliverance is a Spirit-given repentance in which they look on the One they pierced and mourn, and so are cleansed.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter predicts what God will do; it does not merely command. Yet it holds out the abiding pattern of all true conversion: grace poured out, eyes opened to the crucified Lord, honest grief over sin, and mercy that meets the mourner.

debates.question: Who is the pierced One, and how does the shift from Me to Him work?

views.label: The LORD Himself, pierced in the person of the Son
views.case: God says they pierced Me, then mourn for Him; the New Testament resolves this in the incarnation, where the God of Israel is pierced in the flesh of Jesus, whom John 19:37 names as the fulfillment.

views.label: The LORD's representative, closely identified with Him
views.case: Some read the pierced One as God's appointed messenger or shepherd, so bound to the LORD that an offense against him is an offense against God; the New Testament still directs this to Christ.

debates.bearing: Both agree the pierced One is looked upon in saving faith and mourned in repentance, and both are gathered up in the crucified Jesus of John 19.

debates.question: Does the mourning describe a future turning of ethnic Israel, or the wider people of God?

views.label: A future national repentance of Israel
views.case: Read with Romans 11, the passage foretells a day when the veil is lifted and the Jewish people look to their pierced Messiah, mourn, and are saved, family by family.

views.label: The ingathering of all God's people to the crucified Christ
views.case: Others hear a pattern already begun at Pentecost and continuing wherever the Spirit convicts hearts and turns them to the pierced Lord, Jew and Gentile alike, until every eye sees Him.

debates.bearing: Either way the saving look is at Christ crucified and the response is Spirit-wrought mourning that leads to cleansing, not despair.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on four words. Jerusalem is set as an even ma'amasah, a burdensome stone that ruptures everyone who tries to lift it, so the city's safety rests not in her walls but in the God who makes her heavy. Then salvation is described as an outpouring of chen, grace, a Spirit-given favor that softens hearts of stone; the mourning that follows is grace's fruit, not its price. They look on the One they daqar, pierced, the same verb John 19:37 uses of the spear driven into the side of the crucified Jesus, so that Zechariah's pierced LORD and John's pierced Christ are one. And they grieve for Him as for a yachid, an only child, the very word spoken of Isaac, Abraham's only son, foreshadowing the only begotten Son given up for us. Every term drives toward the cross and the repentant look that saves.

typology.type: The One pierced and mourned as an only child
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the only begotten Son, pierced on the cross and looked upon in faith, John 19:37 and 3:16.

typology.type: Jerusalem the immovable stone that breaks the nations
typology.fulfillment: Christ the rejected stone become the cornerstone and the crushing stone, Matthew 21:42-44 and 1 Peter 2:6-8.

typology.type: The outpoured spirit of grace and prayer
typology.fulfillment: The Spirit poured out at Pentecost who convicts of sin and draws hearts to the crucified Lord, Acts 2 and John 16:8.

study.christ: This chapter reaches its summit in a single line that the New Testament plants at the foot of the cross: they will look on Me, the One they have pierced. The words are startling, for it is the LORD who speaks, and He says they pierced Me, and yet in the same breath the people mourn for Him. The mystery is opened at Calvary, where the God of Israel is pierced in the person of His incarnate Son. John, standing as an eyewitness beneath the cross, watches the soldier's spear go into Jesus' side and declares that this happened to fulfill the Scripture, they will look on the One they have pierced, John 19:37. The grief Zechariah describes is the grief of those who at last see what they did to the beloved, the only child, the firstborn Son, and it is not a hopeless grief; it is the mourning of repentance, drawn out by an outpoured spirit of grace. And, crucially, this mourning flows straight into the opened fountain of chapter 13, so that the very ones who pierced Him are the ones He cleanses. Here is the gospel in prophecy: the pierced Lord turns the guilt of His murderers into the occasion of their salvation, so that all who look on Christ crucified and mourn their sin find not condemnation but a fountain washed open by His blood.
study.god: The God of this chapter is the Creator who stretches out the heavens, lays the foundation of the earth, and forms the human spirit within, and He turns every ounce of that majesty toward His people. He makes a small city an immovable stone, strikes the massed armies with panic and blindness, and makes the feeblest defender fight like David. Yet His greatest act is not military but gracious: He pours out a spirit of grace and prayer that opens blind eyes and softens hard hearts. This is a God who saves not merely by conquering His enemies but by converting them, who so binds Himself to the pierced One that the wound His people inflict becomes the wound through which they are healed. He is holy, for sin is real and grief over it is right; He is merciful, for the mourning He produces is answered by cleansing, not wrath; and He is sovereign, for the piercing His people freely and guiltily chose stands at the center of His saving plan.

crossReferences.note: Cites this prophecy over the crucified Jesus: they will look on the One they have pierced.
Every eye will see Him, including those who pierced Him, and all the tribes will mourn, echoing this text.
A hardening on part of Israel until the fullness of the Gentiles, and then a turning of Israel to their Deliverer.
A crowd in Jerusalem, told they crucified the Lord, is cut to the heart and calls out for mercy, a first fulfillment of the mourning.

study.oneStory: The nations gather to destroy the city of God, but God makes her an immovable stone and defends her. The turning point of the whole story is not a battle but a look: a Spirit-given gaze at the One they pierced, and a flood of repentant tears. That look is fixed at Golgotha, where the God of Israel, in His Son, is pierced for our transgressions, and the mourning of the guilty becomes the doorway into the fountain that cleanses them.

quiz.q: What does God make Jerusalem for the gathered nations?

options.text: A cup of drunkenness and an immovable, heavy stone that injures all who try to lift it.
options.note: Verses 2 and 3 make the city a staggering cup and a burdensome stone.

options.text: An easy prize quickly overrun.
options.note: The opposite: the nations are broken on her, not victorious over her.

options.text: A place God abandons to the enemy.
options.note: God defends the city; He does not abandon it.

options.text: A neutral bystander in the conflict.
options.note: Jerusalem is the very stone that shatters her attackers.

quiz.q: What does God pour out that brings the people to repentance?

options.text: A spirit of grace and prayer that opens their eyes to the pierced One.
options.note: Verse 10: the mourning is the fruit of an outpoured spirit of grace.

options.text: A spirit of fear that terrifies them into silence.
options.note: It is grace, not mere terror, that produces the saving mourning.

options.text: Wealth and comfort so they forget their guilt.
options.note: The passage produces honest grief over sin, not distraction from it.

options.text: Nothing; they repent entirely on their own.
options.note: Repentance here is a Spirit-given gift, poured out by God.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament use the words about the pierced One?

options.text: John 19:37 applies them to the crucified Jesus, whose side was pierced.
options.note: John cites this verse as fulfilled at the cross.

options.text: They are said to be fulfilled in a battle of the Maccabees.
options.note: The New Testament ties them to Christ crucified, not to that conflict.

options.text: They are treated as a mere figure of speech with no fulfillment.
options.note: John reads them as a real prophecy fulfilled in Jesus.

options.text: They are applied to the destruction of Rome.
options.note: John applies the piercing to Jesus on the cross.

quiz.q: How deep is the mourning over the pierced One?

options.text: As deep as grief for an only child and a firstborn son.
options.note: Verse 10 compares it to mourning an only child, the word used of Isaac.

options.text: A brief and passing sadness.
options.note: The grief is the deepest a family can know, not passing.

options.text: Only a formal, outward ritual with no feeling.
options.note: It is heartfelt, Spirit-wrought grief, clan by clan.

options.text: No real sorrow at all.
options.note: The whole land mourns bitterly, family by family.

quiz.q: What is the astonishing thing about who was pierced?

options.text: The LORD says they pierced Me, yet they mourn for Him, fulfilled as God pierced in His Son.
options.note: The shift from Me to Him is answered in the incarnation and the cross.

options.text: It was only a symbolic wound with no real person in view.
options.note: The New Testament sees a real piercing fulfilled in the real Jesus.

options.text: The one pierced was a foreign king.
options.note: The pierced One is identified with the LORD Himself, fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: No one is actually pierced in the passage.
options.note: The text plainly speaks of the One they have pierced.

questions: God made a weak city an immovable stone. Where do you need to trust that your safety rests in God rather than in your own strength?
The saving turn was a look at the pierced One. Have you truly looked on Christ crucified, or only glanced past the cross?
The mourning here is Spirit-given grace, not mere guilt. How does it change repentance to know that even your sorrow for sin is a gift from God?
They grieved for the pierced One as for an only child. Does your sin ever move you to that kind of honest grief, and then to His mercy?
God turned the wound His people inflicted into the source of their cleansing. Where do you need to believe that God can redeem even your worst failures?

translationNotes.note: Heavy stone renders even ma'amasah, a burdensome boulder; the image is of a weight too great to lift, so that all who heave it are ruptured in the effort.

The Hebrew reads look on Me, the One they have pierced, then shifts to mourn for Him; a few witnesses read look to the one, but the received text and John 19:37 support the reading that identifies the pierced One with the LORD Himself.
The wailing of Hadad-rimmon in the plain of Megiddo likely recalls the national mourning over the death of good King Josiah, a byword for deep public grief.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Zechariah 12:1-14. All 14 verses reproduced in order. No em or en dashes in the source verses of this chapter; footnote markers omitted from the running text.
